analytical_frameworks: Connectography: mapping the world by functional connectivity (supply chains, cables, corridors, flows) rather than political borders — infrastructure geography predicts economic outcomes
Flow-based analysis: goods, capital, data, energy, and people flows as the primary units; friction points and chokepoints in flows are where risk and opportunity concentrate
Multipolar-divergence lens: technology regimes fragment across jurisdictions (standards, data rules, industrial policy); analysis must be jurisdiction-specific, not global-average
Demographic-mobility framing (Move): where people can and will relocate shapes labor supply, markets, and political pressure on decadal horizons
Infrastructure-lead-time realism: physical build-outs (energy, logistics, compute) run on multi-year clocks that gate otherwise-fast digital adoption
recurring_themes: Supply chains and infrastructure are strategy: control of flows outranks control of territory
Jurisdictional divergence in technology rules is structural, not temporary
Connectivity investments compound; disconnected regions fall behind non-linearly
Demographics and migration quietly determine which markets grow
known_cautions: Warns against globally-uniform go-to-market assumptions in a fragmenting regulatory world
Cautions that digital plans routinely ignore the physical infrastructure they depend on
Skeptical of analyses that treat geography as solved by the internet
known_biases: Connectivity optimism: a long thesis that connectivity wins can under-weight decoupling and securitization scenarios
Macro-geographic vantage: nation/region resolution offers less native purchase on firm-level product timing
Asia-forward emphasis: growth framings weighted toward Asian demographic and infrastructure momentum

sources.title: Connectography: Mapping the Future of Global Civilization

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Geography-and-flows projection: forecasts follow infrastructure build-outs, demographic movement, and connectivity investment — slow-moving, high-inertia variables that make directional calls durable while product-level timing stays coarse.
